title: Host / Hostess
metaDescription: Write a standout host/hostess resume with actionable tips, real bullet examples, and ATS keywords. Land your next hospitality role.
h1: Host/Hostess Resume Guide: What Hiring Managers Actually Want
intro: Your host/hostess resume is your first impression—and in hospitality, first impressions matter. Whether you're breaking into the industry or moving up to a busier restaurant, we'll show you how to highlight the customer-facing and organizational skills that get you hired.
audience: Entry-level job seekers, career switchers from retail or admin roles, and experienced hosts/hostesses looking to move to higher-volume establishments.

topSkills.name: Guest Relations & Communication
topSkills.why: Hosts are the face of the restaurant; managers need proof you can greet guests warmly, handle complaints gracefully, and represent the brand.

topSkills.name: Point of Sale (POS) Systems
topSkills.why: Most modern restaurants use software like Toast, OpenTable, or Square; familiarity with reservation and seating software is table stakes.

topSkills.name: Reservation Management
topSkills.why: Knowing how to manage waitlists, honor hold times, and balance walk-ins shows operational awareness that keeps the restaurant running smoothly.

topSkills.name: Multitasking Under Pressure
topSkills.why: Friday nights are chaos; managers want evidence you stay calm, prioritize, and don't get flustered when the door is slammed.

topSkills.name: Attention to Detail
topSkills.why: Seating guests correctly, capturing accurate reservation info, and maintaining the host stand reflects professionalism and reduces server friction.

topSkills.name: Phone Etiquette & Booking
topSkills.why: Hosts handle reservations and inquiries over the phone; clear, courteous communication directly impacts customer retention and covers.

topSkills.name: Restaurant Procedures & Service Standards
topSkills.why: Knowledge of menu, allergen protocols, and house policies shows you're ready to answer guest questions and uphold the restaurant's reputation.

topSkills.name: Team Collaboration
topSkills.why: Hosts work closely with servers, bussers, and kitchen staff; managers want to see you're a team player who makes everyone's job easier.

topSkills.name: Cash Handling & Money Management
topSkills.why: Many hosts collect or verify payments; accuracy and honesty with cash builds trust and prevents costly errors.

sampleBullets.weak: Greeted guests and seated them at tables.
sampleBullets.strong: Greeted 150–200+ guests nightly with genuine warmth, managed waitlist of 20–30 parties during peak hours, and maintained average table turn time of 90 minutes to maximize covers.
sampleBullets.lesson: Specificity and metrics (numbers of guests, table management, efficiency) prove impact and scale; action verbs like 'managed' and 'maintained' show ownership.

sampleBullets.weak: Used the restaurant's POS system and took reservations.
sampleBullets.strong: Mastered Toast POS system for seating, reservation logging, and payment entry; resolved 95%+ of guest booking issues on-site without manager escalation.
sampleBullets.lesson: Naming the specific software and quantifying accuracy/resolution rate demonstrates technical competence and problem-solving, not just task completion.

sampleBullets.weak: Worked with the team to make guests happy.
sampleBullets.strong: Coordinated seamlessly with servers and bussers during 150-cover Friday services, flagged dietary restrictions and special requests to kitchen, and received 4.8-star guest feedback on communication.
sampleBullets.lesson: Specific collaboration examples and measurable feedback (ratings, volume handled) show concrete value to the team, not vague 'teamwork.'

commonMistakes.mistake: Listing 'greeting guests' as a bullet point.
commonMistakes.fix: Combine greeting with impact: how many guests, how you managed the flow, what you did to improve their experience or the restaurant's efficiency.

commonMistakes.mistake: No mention of POS systems or reservation software.
commonMistakes.fix: Name the specific platforms you've used (OpenTable, Toast, Yelp Reservations, etc.) and highlight any advanced features or troubleshooting you handled.

commonMistakes.mistake: Ignoring the operational side of the role.
commonMistakes.fix: Show you understand restaurant rhythms: table turnover, capacity management, wait time communication, and how you support the back-of-house.

commonMistakes.mistake: Using passive language like 'responsible for' or 'involved in.'
commonMistakes.fix: Lead with action verbs: 'managed,' 'coordinated,' 'optimized,' 'resolved,' 'maintained'—language that shows agency and ownership.

commonMistakes.mistake: No acknowledgment of multi-venue or upscale experience.
commonMistakes.fix: If you've worked fine dining, high-volume chains, or events, highlight the added complexity (wine knowledge, dress code, timing precision, reservation depth) to show you can scale.

structureTips: Lead with a brief professional summary or objective that speaks directly to hospitality and the type of establishment (e.g., 'Welcoming Host with 3 years of fast-casual and fine-dining experience; expert at managing 100+ reservations weekly and delivering 5-star first impressions').
Place POS and software skills prominently in a dedicated 'Technical Skills' or 'Key Skills' section near the top—don't bury them in bullet points.
Use reverse chronological order for job history, but for each role, lead with one or two metrics-driven bullets about guest volume, efficiency, or satisfaction before moving to team/procedural wins.
Include a brief 'Hospitality Certifications' section if you have Food Handler, ServSafe, or customer service training—even small credentials signal professionalism.
salarySignal: Entry-level US host/hostess salaries typically range from $24,000 to $32,000 annually, plus tips and bonuses; hourly rates average $13–$16 before gratuity, with higher-volume and upscale venues paying more.

faq.q: Should I include cash handling on a host/hostess resume?
faq.a: Yes, if you've done it. Many hosts verify guest payments or handle petty cash. Mention it briefly as evidence of trustworthiness and accuracy—e.g., 'Verified and processed payments for 50–75 covers nightly with 100% accuracy.'

faq.q: How do I show I can handle a high-volume restaurant?
faq.a: Use specific numbers: covers served, guests seated per hour, peak-shift volume, and waitlist size. Include metrics on efficiency (table turn time, average wait managed) to show you thrive under pressure, not just survive it.

faq.q: What if I've never used a fancy POS system?
faq.a: Name the systems you *have* used, even simple ones like Square or Yelp Reservations, and highlight your ability to learn quickly. If you're applying to a place with a specific POS, mention in your cover letter that you're eager to master it.

faq.q: Should I mention dealing with difficult guests on my resume?
faq.a: Not in a complaint way. Instead, frame it as conflict resolution or problem-solving: 'Resolved guest complaints and seating delays with calm, solution-focused communication, maintaining 90%+ satisfaction scores.'

faq.q: How important is fine-dining or event experience?
faq.a: It's a plus, especially if you're moving to upscale venues—mention it. Fine dining requires knowledge of dress codes, wine basics, and precision timing, which separates you from casual-only hosts and shows you can handle complexity.
